topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the schematic spread across his workbench, his hand frozen halfway through adjusting a resistor. The faint scent of solder and old coffee hung heavy in the air of the cluttered workshop. He traced a line on the blueprint, his brow furrowing deeper with each pass. Then, his gaze drifted across the room to the adjacent station, where a figure—a person he vaguely recognized from the shared lab space—was meticulously assembling the exact same circuit board. Elias blinked, a slow, deliberate motion. He leaned closer, his breath catching in his throat. The components, the wiring diagram, the very configuration of the prototype—it was a mirror image. A strange, cold wave washed over him, making his fingers tremble slightly. He slowly pushed back from the table, the sound of his chair scraping the concrete floor seeming deafening in the sudden silence.

[story 2]

"No way," Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible over the hum of the ventilation system. She watched Mark, across the narrow aisle, delicately place a microchip onto his custom PCB. She had been agonizing over this design for weeks, wrestling with the optimal power distribution, and now, seeing his hand move with such practiced familiarity, a sudden vacancy opened in her mind. Her jaw went slack. She took a hesitant step toward the shared display screen, her eyes darting back and forth between her own finalized plans and Mark’s tangible creation. A faint tremor ran through her hands; she clutched the edge of the counter until her knuckles turned white. It felt as if the ground had tilted beneath her feet.

[story 3]

I picked up the final prototype casing. It felt weighty, solid, exactly as I had envisioned it. I turned, expecting to see the usual chaotic sprawl of tools and discarded prototypes, but instead, I found another person hunched over a desk, their concentration absolute. They were soldering the same tiny connector, the placement of every component identical to mine. My mouth went dry. I let the casing slip slightly in my grip, and the sound of the plastic hitting the metal surface was startlingly loud. I stood there, rooted to the spot, unable to process the sheer improbability of the situation. My vision seemed to narrow, focusing intensely on the uncanny symmetry before I could force my feet to move.
